text:
So when Nana Osei-Opare told me he was interested in reprinting an article I had written in graduate school, my initial reaction was “Sure why not?” The piece is titled “The Third International and the Struggle for National Liberation in South Africa” and I wrote it for a course at UCLA when I was 23 years old. For this paper I did about a week of reading based on a handful of reference works that drew on newly-opened primary sources – my constraint being that I had no access to South African archives, which even in those days were partially open to outsiders, and I had no chance then of getting to South Africa, anyway. The intention was to write my PhD dissertation on a comparative history of the Black Left in the U.S. South and South Africa. The idea was to be what at the time was known as a “comparativist,” comparing different societies and historical periods that had certain factors in common – in this case “racism” in a colonial or neo-colonial context – in the expectation of shedding light not so much on each in particular but on the overarching category. Even then that project seemed somewhat fanciful, given that in 1985 the apartheid president, P. W. Botha, had recently declared a state of emergency, and I was about to be summoned to speak before a magistrate at the South African Consulate in Beverly Hills over my recent involvement in the demonstrations held regularly in front of the consulate.

In my dorm room back in 1991, I decided to spend some time churning out this four-page essay for some reason. Upon re-reading it from thirty years on, some parts read more than a bit red-faced, especially when I remember how I used to speak – lots of official, Soviet-flavored “Marxist-Leninist” phraseology. As if that mattered much anyway. The Comintern archives were still sealed, at least until a few years after the Soviet bloc collapse of 1989. But aside from the obnoxious tautology of teenage Marxist-Leninism, the essay in retrospect holds up in a lot of respects. I remember my intellectual hero, Cedric Robinson, would have just published his magnum opus, Black Marxism (1983) just a few years before, and I would argue, using lots of Black Marxist ideological language that then only few people in my generation could fully appreciate, that the world’s first Black radical tradition was growing – with roots in “African redemptionism” of some kind, the various rebellions against the 1913 South African Land Act that caused white settler agriculturalists so much unease, as well as the first hints of African nationalism in the broader world – and that these emerging black radical perspectives all already predated the founding of the Communist Party of South Africa (CPSA) in some way, in that what little I could read about their platform they seemed to have had their focus far too heavily on the radical white working class in the context of the Rand Revolt labor strikes of 1922, the (Communist) party’s placards trumpeting “Workers of the World Unite and Fight for a WHITE SOUTH AFRICA!” the sort of thing that can still raise my blood pressure to this day.
explanation: The text shows detailed, date-stamped personal recollections and a reflective, informal tone typical of human memoir writing. This level of specific context is less common in AI-generated text.
